sentences.text: All right, we have now moved to September, 1936.
And we moved to a place called Heemstede in Holland.
It's, it's a small town as a suburb of Haarlem, which itself is about 15 miles west of Amsterdam.
Basically, Heemstede is very near to the seaside.
A famous seaside place in that area is called Zandvoort.
So we were within a few miles of Zandvoort seaside.
No.
[LAUGHS] Basically, I suppose, we said hello and all the usual kind of things because we hadn't seen him for six months.
And then we were, no, no doubt, very glad to see him again.
But I can't remember anything about that in, in particular.
It's a question nobody's ever asked me, actually.
So we, we set up house in Heemstede.
We lived in number 11, Johannes Vermeerstraat.
And we've been back to Heemstede.
And we're amazed how small and titchy little place it is.
But, in those days, it looked quite big to us, not perhaps compared with Berlin and London, where we'd lived before.
But, nevertheless, we thought it was big-ish.
And we picked up on school again.
Most of my life seemed to revolve about going to different schools.
And, of course, yet another language-- Dutch, which, of course, we had no idea.
We could speak German.
And we could speak a bit of English.
But we couldn't speak Dutch at all.
But there, especially at school, there was only Dutch.
So Ruti and me, we both went to school there.
And we had to learn it.
And no doubt, we picked it up very quickly because, again, we have the school reports.
And I can say that, in Dutch language, very soon, I was up with the best, really.
So I must have been fairly good at picking up languages.
On the other hand, I've recently discovered that I never learned the bases of any language.
I've picked up all languages on the basis of listening to other people and imitating them.
And in that way, maybe I am fortunate in not having too many accents in most of the languages I can now speak.
But I've never really learned the grammar.
And in writing my book, I find this a quite a handicap, actually, that I never really quite know how to write certain things the proper way.
But Holland was probably the best years of my life, or of my childhood, certainly, kind of thing.
We, we soon made lots of friends in the street and also at school.
And in those days, apart from school, which I used to enjoy very much like all the other schools before-- but there, I really got down to the sports business, which maybe I'd learned from Rudi.
But Holland, of course, was an ideal place for swimming, first of all.
Everybody went swimming.
And you had to have certificates and diplomas in order to be allowed to go into the deep end.
And you had to pass these examinations.
And you had to swim-- be able to swim with all your clothes on, and all those kind of things.
I remember all that.
And I have the certificates here to show you that I passed them.
And you had to have cycling-- cycling was another big thing Holland, of course.
We all had bicycles, even father, mother, Ruti.
We all had a bicycle.
And you had to pass tests.
And you got a certificate that you were-- proficiency type of-- proficient in, in riding bicycles kind of thing.
So we, we did a lot of cycling to school and at the weekends with my mother.
Not so much father-- he wasn't very keen on cycling.
But my mother did.
And we used to cycle to the seaside, to Zandvoort, by the beach.
And we, we had a very good time there, really.
What did you do at the seaside?
I think we went swimming and, and just playing around, like children do on the beach.
And there were lots of others.
And maybe have all these funny foods that they have at the-- at the sea, pancakes and poffertjes and all sorts of strange things.
And seaside life, it was very nice.
In those days, we didn't have the big holidays anymore like we had in Germany.
I think we-- my parents probably lost a lot of any money that they might have had.
And, and the job wasn't anything like as good that my father had-- that he had in Berlin.
So I wouldn't say that we were poor.
But we were-- we were not as middle-class, affluent as we had been in Berlin.
And we didn't go on any holidays outside of Holland.

And, actually, another thing that I always forget in the story is that my father also brought his four grandparents-- his parents and my mother's parents-- all those four grandparents from Nuremberg and Heidelberg.
He brought them all to Holland to Heemstede.
And, and they lived in another house not very far from us, the four grandparents together, in Heemstede.
So by the time he'd accomplished all that and all the financial involvement of all those things, we-- well, were not very affluent those days.
But that didn't affect us as kids.
We weren't bothered, really.
We were quite happy.
And from school, we had excursions to zoos and to airports and all those sort of things I remember.
And we have photographs of all those sort of happy days in Holland.
And that's where I started to play football and even cricket, you know, which was not really much played in Holland.
But there was a cricket club there.
And I joined there.
And, as I say, the swimming was a big feature of everyday life and the cycling.
And in the winter, there was loads of ice skating.
That was another thing that I was very keen on-- was ice skating.
On the rivers and the canals, and the lakes.
And everything, everything tended to freeze over most winters.
And it was great fun to go out on those.
The Dutch had some very funny skates, these wooden things with a little steel blade that you just tied underneath your normal shoes.
And you would just take them off again and put them on to go ice skating, and then take them off and walk the rest of the way.
I remember, in particular, one of my few things I remember of those days is that I went on ice skates all the way to school one day.
It was a very long way around various canals and rivers.
But it was very unusual for a big river that you had to go along, for that to freeze over one winter.
And so I could get to school.
As I say, it was probably 10 times as long as the normal route.
But I did it on ice skates all the way to school and back again kind of thing.
And, yeah, that was something that I remember very well.
So yes, it was good.
And we really enjoyed ourselves in Holland.
The interesting thing is-- although at the time we didn't realize it-- is that we didn't have any Jewish friends at all.
We, again, seemed to live a very secular life.
I, I don't remember ever having gone to a synagogue.
I don't remember any Friday nights and, and all those sort of things.
As it happened, there, there was a Jewish family living next to us and another Jewish family living opposite us.
But I still believe that there weren't many Jews [LAUGHS] in Heemstede in that area.
But I may well have been wrong.
It's interesting that the family that lived opposite us in that street, in Johannes Vermeerstraat in Heemstede, were later on with us in Belsen.
And they were one of the very few families that got out of Belsen.
They were exchanged.
And they went to Palestine.
And we've heard of them after the war, you know, that they actually did get to Palestine and then lived there.
I think our parents must have told them.
I mean, we obviously knew that we were Jewish because, well, we were refugees from Germany.
And therefore, I would imagine that both those families also were refugees from Germany because their name-- Mainz was the one, and Dietz was the other one.
Mainz certainly is a very Germanic name, almost the same as Oppenheimer.
In fact, Mainz is very near to Oppenheimer in Germany.
So I, I, I think, by the name, we probably deduced it wasn't a very Dutch name.
So that--
Maybe, or maybe my parents told us that they were Jewish like us, kind of thing.
And maybe-- you're right.
Maybe we did know more about Judaism, and we did do more Judaism than I can remember.
But I, I just can't recall it.
I found out later on that we did have Megillahs and Torahs at home and all sorts of Jewish artifacts.

Because later on, when we put all our things away and gave all our things to friends to keep for us while we were in the camps, and when we got them back, there were a lot of things like that that we did possess.
But I just don't remember that we ever used them.
Maybe we did.
No.
I had-- I had many friends.
And I would say that there probably was one or two amongst them who were Jewish.
I don't know quite how we found that they were, but generally not.
It was certainly not a Jewish area there and-- or a Jewish school or anything like that.
Very few Jews, I would say.
And then we never talked about Judaism.
So-- we only talked about school activities and sports, really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But those were the good days, really.
And we were very happy in Heemstede.
Again, that I liked it.
I enjoyed it.
And I did well in, in the class.
And I remember some of the friends from school have photographs of school, two friends in particular, who both had a birthday on the same day, 23rd of August, which I somehow always remember.
And in fact, I'm still in contact with those two boys from those days.
When you see them on the photograph, it's very strange because the one that, even in those days, [NON-ENGLISH], he had totally white hair.
And the other boy, [?
Jurgen ?] Carmelo, he was a Negro.
We always called him a Negro.
I mean, in those days, there was nothing wrong with [LAUGHS] calling him Negro.
Because Holland, like Britain, had a big empire, actually, which not many people seem to understand or know.
They had lots of possessions in both East Indies and the West Indies-- the West Indies, Java, Sumatra, and all that sort of thing.
That's all now Indonesia.
That all belonged to Holland before the war.
So our lessons in geography, et cetera, included all those kind of countries.
That was all part of Holland.
And the same, the Dutch West Indies-- sorry, the other one is the East Indies.
In West Indies, in Guyana, Curacao, and Suriname, for example, that was all part of Holland.
And, and this boy, Carmelo, he came from Suriname or Curacao, one, one of those.
And he was brown, black.
And, and he was the only one in the whole school, and then one of the few, actually, [LAUGHS] in Holland or Heemstede.
In that area, everybody knew him kind of thing.
And I think he stood out.
But nobody was bothered about him.
And he was one of my best friends.
Those two were.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Strangely enough, Carmelo still lives in the same house that he lived in-- [LAUGHTER] --in those days.
That's how I found him, kind of-- 13 [NON-ENGLISH] in Haarlem, really.
And after 60 years, he's still in the same house.
And he still looks the same-- brown.
And yes, it was good, really.
Unfortunately-- well, another good thing was our father often took us to restaurants in Amsterdam.
He-- because he worked in Amsterdam, he went by tram every day from Heemstede to Amsterdam.
He knew Amsterdam pretty well.
And we particularly developed a taste for various restaurants in Amsterdam.
And and that was a treat for us to go on the tram and be treated to some of these restaurants and, in particular, have very nice ice creams and things like that kind of thing.
So that's some of the things that we remember.
We didn't have a car or anything like that.
We had bicycles.
And we moved around by tram in Holland.
However, all these happy events changed on the 10th of May, 1940.
I mean, these are some-- when, when people ask me, you know, what do you remember more than-- one day that I always remember is the 10th of May, 1940.
Because that was the day when the Germans invaded Holland--
--and Belgium and France.
The-- I remember quite a lot about that particular day, kind of thing, in particular, that we could hear all the planes overhead.
And we could see the parachutes coming down out of the sky.
I think that's something that we, or that I, will always remember.
And as schoolboys, of course, we were all watching kind of thing with interest.
We didn't realize the implications of it, I don't suppose.
But all the school kids together, we all used to go and, and stand there and watch all the parachuters coming down.
And there were roadblocks set up all over Holland, apparently.
But there were some near, near us.
And we went-- we all went to this roadblock.
Because what, what we hadn't realized-- we'd only recently find out-- is that there was a big Fifth Column in Holland.
Many Germans had infiltrated into Holland before the invasion, before the war.
And they were all coming out of the woodwork on the 10th of May, when the Germans invaded, as saboteurs, to sabotage behind the lines.
So now I found out why they had these roadblocks.
I mean, I just remember that they had the roadblocks and that, every time you went past the roadblocks, you had to pronounce the names of various Dutch towns, like [SPEAKING DUTCH].
And nobody except a Dutchman could pronounce those, you see.
Germans couldn't pronounce that at all.
And that's how they find out which were infiltrators, saboteurs, Fifth Columnists.
We just had been with those roadblocks for a while because it was great fun to hear everybody say it, and especially when they found somebody who couldn't say it kind of thing.
And-- but we didn't realize, really, exactly what it was, why it was, until I recently read all this about these Fifth Columnists and saboteurs.
We, we would see, obviously, they were trying to catch Germans.
But all the ones we could see coming out of the sky, they say that a lot of them were dressed in ordinary street clothes and not in uniforms.
But we couldn't see.
They were too far away from us, really, to be able to see whether they were in uniform or whether they were in [NON-ENGLISH] kind of thing.
Yeah, he probably wouldn't have got through the roadblocks.
But he stayed at home, I should think, or didn't go then, yeah.
He wasn't so good.
My mother picked it up quite well.
And, and, us kids, of course, we had no difficulties with it.
We can-- we can still speak it now without an accent.
But yes, he, he wasn't quite so good on the language.
You're right.
Interesting question, kind of thing-- yeah, you ask a lot of interesting questions I haven't really thought about-- more food for thought, but-- yes.
So basically, the war lasted only five days because the Germans bombed Rotterdam.
They flattened Rotterdam.
And they threatened to do the same to Amsterdam.
So Holland capitulated, partly also because of all this intrigue behind the lines.
The, the Dutch apparently flooded a large area of Holland in order to stop the invasion.
But the Germans had already got to the other side of it by all these Fifth Columnists and the paratroopers, et cetera.
So the Dutch defense plan just didn't work at all.
And when they threatened to flatten Amsterdam by bombing it, like they'd done in Poland, all that sort of thing, the Dutch gave up.
And within five days, they capitulated.
And the Germans took charge.
And this is, of course, when all the trouble started.
I, I gather that many people did try and get away, get out of Holland, get to England in those days.
But I can't remember us making any attempt like that at all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So for, for a time, I suppose, at first, life went on as, as before.
And we didn't really feel the Nazi rules.

But it, it was quite strange that one of the first effects that, that we had on these racial laws-- of which there were more and more could have come later on-- there was one law, which I've never, ever seen mentioned by anybody.
And that was the one that affected us at first, within six months.
And that was that Jews were not to live-- not allowed to live by the seaside.
And this was obviously because the Germans were starting to build fortifications there in order to repel the British or attack England or something.
But anyway, they built-- of course, as we found out later on-- big fortifications and guns and placements and all that sort of thing by Zandvoort and all the way there, along the beach.
And they presumably thought that Jews were not wanted in that area.
I mean, I'm surprised they didn't let anybody live in that area.
But, but it wasn't-- everybody else was all right.
But Jews were not allowed to live in that area.
And we had to move.
This was before.
Later on, all Jews had to move to Amsterdam.
And then we were well aware of that.
And we were caught by that as well.
But this was very early in September.
So only six months after the invasion, really, we had to move from Heemstede, where we'd lived for four years very happily.
We had to move away.
And we moved to a place called Naarden, which is just east of Amsterdam, near Hilversum, which is better known, because that's the radio station.
So we went live in Naarden, in [DUTCH], number 31.

Naarden is, in Dutch, a [DUTCH], I never really worked out, exactly, what the English word for that is-- like a fortress, a medieval fortress town with a moat all the way around it and, and a wall around it for, for defending the town.
And that's what it was.
And on the inside, there were loads of houses and a school and a church and all that sort of thing.
And we went to school inside this fortress area in the middle of Naarden.
So we went to, to yet another school-- same language, fortunately.
And we stayed there for, I think, the best part of a year, in Naarden.
Now, in Naarden, this was the first time that Ruti and me remember of having religious activities.
Ruti believes that my mother conducted Jewish classes at our home in this [DUTCH].
I don't remember too much about it.
But Ruti says we did.
Because now, there, there were more Jews, really, in the area than there had been in Heemstede, in Hilversum, in Bussum, kind of thing.
And there were various people who were conducting Jewish classes at home.
I think it was also-- the Nazi occupation tended to bring the Jews closer together with each other.
Before that, you know, we'd just been Germans.
And we'd just been Dutch.
But now, with the, the Germans in charge, I think we became more Jewish.
We've-- we were beginning to feel that we were different from the others and, and not really the same as we'd always thought before.
Nothing.
[LAUGHS] Ruti just says that we had it.
And I presume we learned about the festivals.
And maybe we started to learn about Hebrew.

Because it is interesting that, at the beginning of the war-- the 10th of May 1940, which for us is the beginning of the war-- until the end of it-- when we were liberated in 1945, April-- there were five years.
And when we started, as far as I know, I don't-- I knew nothing about Judaism.
But when I finished, I knew quite a lot about Judaism.
And I learned it all on, on the way, in, in various ways.
And never terribly organized, I don't think, but by these sort of classes, maybe, that my mother took in, in our home and again later on in various places in the camps, kind of thing.
There were the odd bits here and there.
And we picked it all up.
And, and by the time we finished, we knew a lot about Judaism.
But we never really [LAUGHS] remember having been taught it properly, probably-- properly, anyway.
I just remember that Naarden was yet another good place for sports.
And, and-- [LAUGHTER] --there, [LAUGHS] all my life seemed to revolve about going to school, which I again did.
And we again have reports from the days that I went to school there.
And I got good marks, even in Dutch, from this school in Naarden.
And-- but there, I remember playing lots of football, in particular.
And I played not just for the school team.
I played for a local town team there and remember some of the events there and scoring goals and all that sort of thing.
And I also remember the ice skating again.
Naarden was an ideal place for ice skating because it had this moat all the way around, this fortress.
So that froze in the winter that we were there.
And it was an ideal place for skating around and playing ice hockey and all sorts of things like that-- really great.
We had a big swimming-- there were swimming baths, of course, everywhere in Heemstede.
I didn't mention that we had several swimming baths where we went regularly, all the time.
And we spent all our time not swimming, really, but diving in and climbing out and diving in again and playing tag and being a nuisance kind of thing.
In Haarlem, I remember playing football in the swimming bowl-- in the swimming pool, which you were not supposed to kind of thing.
And I fell through a great, big, glass panel.
And I had a big cut in my arm.
And I was taken to a hospital.
And you can still see the marks of the stitches that I had from there.
And another time, we were always diving kind of thing.
We were messing around on the diving board, the 10-meter board.
And one day, I was pushed off it onto the stones below kind of thing, not into the water, but onto the tiles kind of thing.
And I had various injuries then and went to the hospital again.
But yeah, those are things I remember.
But in Naarden there was even more of it.
And perhaps I was older.
By that time, I was 13, 14-- no, 13, something like that.
So maybe I have a better memory of it by then.
So, as I say, the ice skating and football, I remember very well-- cycling, of course, always.
Very little about life at home.
My father obviously still worked at this bank at that time in, in Amsterdam.
So he traveled to Amsterdam every day and worked there.
I don't believe that my mother ever really worked.
Of course, we had Eve, who was quite young, six, something like that, five, four.
And so I think mother spent most of the time at home.
I mean, wives didn't go out to work too much, in those days, anyway.
And I can't remember really much about home life.
It-- well, I can't even remember ever listening to the radio, for example.
Obviously, there was no television or things like that.
So-- and I can't remember much about newspapers.
The other thing-- I remember that we must have read newspapers-- is that I was very interested in cycling and the Tour de France, for example.
And I was avidly following the Dutch rider's progress in the Tour de France in those days.
So I suppose we must have seen newspapers or, or something like that.
And we also read quite a lot of books.
And, and we had strange picture books, where you could sort of flash through them.
And it showed you a story kind of thing of criminal detective stories and things like that by flashing these books, very strange.
Do you mean like a cartoon?
Yes, yes, that sort of thing, yes.
Now you mention it, I remember, yes, some of those sort of things.
But basically, life at home, not very much.
I mean, I never really thought about whether I had my own room, or whether I slept in the same room with Ruti.
I can't remember any of those sort of things.
Yes.
They must also have moved from Heemstede.
And they were very elderly.
They went to Amsterdam.
There was a place, an old age home called [DUTCH] in Amsterdam.
And all the elderly Jews went to that place, really, yes.
So I suppose we-- at that time, we were still allowed to travel in Holland.
So I suppose we did go and visit them from time to time from Naarden to Amsterdam and see them there, yes.
No, all that came a bit later, really.
I mean, yeah, we're now coming to the time, really.
And this is when I prefer to use my notes, really.
But, basically, all those restrictions now appeared.
And it's difficult to get them all in the right order.
But first of all, all Jews had to register.
And we all had to carry identity cards.
And the identity cards had a big J.
And we had a photograph.
Young children didn't have to have them.
And I can't remember now whether I and Ruti had them, at what age you started with them.
But I think we did have them.
But the actual ones that we still have here, today, for you are, are the identity cards of my two paternal grandparents.
And there, you can see very clearly their photograph, their fingerprint, and a big J by the side of their name.
And also, you can see that their names were Josef and Meta.
But the Germans made us-- all, all Jews had to take on the name of 'Israel' if you were male and 'Sarah' if you were female.
I mean, I never understood the reason for this.
I mean, a ridiculous idea kind of thing.
I don't know what it meant.
Nobody called them Israel or Sarah.
But on this identity card, it's typed, Josef.
And in ink, it's written, Israel, by the side of it.
And, and the same for-- Meta, typed, Sarah, in ink written by the side of it.
So that was obviously added later on to the identity cards, when everybody had to carry these identity cards with a J.
And they had to take these names of Israel and Sarah.
